data.title: U.S. shale production likely to slow due to oil price weakness, UBS says
data.description:
Crude oil futures eked out slim gains Monday, as the Trump administration's temporary relief from tariffs on electronic goods was offset by a 150K bbl/day cut in OPEC's demand growth outlook, underscoring the market's troubled outlook and high level of uncertainty.

Oil prices backed off during the day as equities trimmed some gains, BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler said, believing the oil market "lost its luster with the selloff in equities, and there's still a lot of demand concerns in the market because there are still a lot of what ifs and uneasiness over the tariffs."

Oil appears to have stabilized following Trump's tariff rollout, but at levels that underscore the market's bearish view on demand, according to John Coleman of Sparta Commodities.

"Trade war frictions continue to weigh on WTI, as retaliatory tariffs and likely more policy uncertainty to come force it to price far more competitively than usual," Coleman wrote, adding that WTI last week appeared to offer the best relative value globally, but the U.S. benchmark lost some of its competitive edge with a softening in North Sea prices and a near-collapse of Middle Eastern premium.

OPEC cut its global economic growth expectations, saying in its monthly oil market report that it now forecasts oil demand to grow by 1.3M bbl/day this year, below its previous outlook for 1.45M bbl/day, and lowered its forecasts for world economic growth this year and next, citing U.S. trade tariffs.

OPEC's oil demand view is still at the higher end of industry forecasts and it expects oil use will continnue to climb for years, unlike the International Energy Agency, which sees demand peaking this decade; its report is due out on Tuesday.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery closed up $0.03 to $61.53/bbl, and front-month June Brent crude (CO1:COM) ended +0.2% to $64.88/bbl, still the fifth lowest settlement value this year for both benchmarks.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI)

U.S. shale producers likely will further slow down their activity as oil prices seem set to remain below $65/bbl, according to UBS, noting the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas Energy Survey indicates shale companies need a $65/bbl average to profitably drill a new well.

"Some indicators... suggest that the oil market remains tight, but tariffs together with the ongoing uncertainty over the U.S. administration's next steps are likely to weigh on economic growth," UBS strategist Giovanni Staunovo wrote, as the bank lowered its global oil demand growth forecast for this year by 400K bbl/day to 800K bbl/day, and now expects an oversupply of 400K bbl/day from 100K bbl/day previously.

Goldman Sachs now sees oil demand rising by only 300K bbl/day between the end of last year and the end of 2025, and cut its global demand growth forecasts for the end of 2026 by 900K bbl/day, with large surpluses of 800K bbl/day in 2025 and 1.4M bbl/day in 2026 continuing to exert downward pressure on oil prices.

The bank forecasts Brent and WTI oil prices to tilt lower, averaging $63/bbl and $59/bbl, respectively, for the rest of 2025, and $58/bbl and $55/bbl, respectively, in 2026.

J.P. Morgan also lowered its oil price forecasts for 2025 and 2026, citing higher production from OPEC+ and weaker demand; the bank now sees 2025 Brent price at $66/bbl compared to its previous outlook for $73/bbl and drops its 2026 target to $58/bbl from $61/bbl, while reducing its 2025 WTI price guidance to $62/bbl from $69/bbl and its 2026 view to $53/bbl from $57/bbl.

data.title: TOMI Environmental projects $1.4M CES deals in 2025 while expanding global partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TOMI Environmental Solutions (TOMZ) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Halden Shane discussed the company's progress in 2024 and early successes in 2025, emphasizing efforts to expand the SteraMist iHP brand globally and secure valuable partnerships in key markets. He highlighted the launch of the SteraMist Integrated System (SIS) and its strong market reception, with a pipeline of opportunities exceeding $1 million since its October 2024 launch.
  • The company has been actively working on seven custom-engineered system (CES) projects, securing its first CES deal for 2025 valued at $3.7 million collectively across all CES and hybrid projects. Discussions are ongoing for 46 additional projects, with plans to close five deals in 2025, estimated at $1.4 million.
  • CFO Nick Jennings confirmed operational cost reductions in 2024, including a 41% cut in R&D expenses and a 20% reduction in consulting expenses. He also noted a targeted effort to return to sustainable positive cash flow.
  • COO E.J. Shane outlined the growth of the SIS platform, including contracts worth $575,000 for three SIS offerings in 2025, as well as the company's successful partnership with PBSC Biotech and six other regional partners in markets like Malaysia, India, and Italy.

Outlook

  • Management anticipates significant revenue contributions in 2025 from SIS and CES projects, complemented by strengthened distribution channels and independent representatives.
  • The company aims to advance its razor/razor-blade business model, with BIT Solution sales showing a 200% year-to-date increase compared to the same period last year.
  • Guidance for ongoing CES and SIS sales suggests a focus on long-term pipeline management, with estimated revenue recognition extending beyond 2025.
  • Plans include exploring partnerships in countries like Panama, Nigeria, Israel, and Australia, further expanding TOMI's global footprint.

Financial Results

  • The company reported $7.7 million in GAAP revenue for 2024, up from $7.4 million in 2023, with a non-GAAP revenue figure of $8 million reflecting a 6% increase. Mobile equipment sales rose 23% year-over-year, while BIT Solution sales grew 9%.
  • GAAP gross margins stood at 46%, down from 58% due to increased inventory reserves. Non-GAAP gross margins were reported at 60%, a slight improvement from 59% in 2023.
  • GAAP operating loss increased to $4.1 million from $3.3 million year-over-year, while non-GAAP operating loss reduced to $1.7 million from $2.8 million.
  • Cash and cash equivalents as of December 31, 2024, were approximately $665,000, with a working capital of $3.8 million.

Q&A

  • Todd Felte from StoneX Wealth Management inquired about guidance for 2025 revenues and margins. CEO Shane stated margins are expected to remain consistent. COO Shane highlighted the growth of the SIS and CES pipelines, projecting ongoing deliveries and servicing into 2026.
  • Felte asked about the potential for improved margins through the razor/razor-blade model. COO Shane confirmed an increasing mix of BIT Solution sales, with a balance between gallon and drum offerings.
  • John Nelson, a private investor, questioned whether tariffs would impact operations. CEO Shane noted no current issues but acknowledged the fluidity of the situation. Nelson also asked about cash adequacy, to which Shane responded that a convertible note raise of $3-$3.5 million is planned to address opportunities.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were cautiously optimistic, with questions focusing on future revenue visibility, margin improvements, and growth potential in key markets like food safety and government contracts.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing strategic partnerships and pipeline growth while addressing challenges like returned orders and financial sustainability.
  • Compared to Q3 2024, the tone was slightly more forward-looking, with greater emphasis on partnerships and expanding market opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for CES and SIS sales in 2025 reflects an expanded focus compared to Q3 2024, with higher projected revenue contributions.
  • Management's tone showed increased confidence in the SIS platform and its market adoption, contrasting with Q3's emphasis on immediate profitability.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from operational efficiency to long-term growth strategies and scalability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted challenges in cash flow for 2024 but expects improvement in 2025 with better execution and progress payments.
  • The company is monitoring potential tariff impacts and changes in regulatory environments for key markets like food safety and medical devices.
  • Analysts raised concerns about returned orders and the company's ability to scale operations with limited cash reserves.

Final Takeaway

TOMI Environmental Solutions is leveraging its expanding SIS and CES platforms to drive growth in 2025, supported by global partnerships and a robust project pipeline. With a focus on operational efficiency and strategic market expansion, the company aims to enhance revenue visibility and scale its razor/razor-blade model. While challenges remain, particularly in cash flow and regulatory environments, management's forward-looking strategy and strong market positioning provide a solid foundation for future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ametek rated Equal-weight in new coverage at Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Ametek (NYSE:AME) on Monday was rated Equal-weight in new research coverage by analysts at financial-services firm Morgan Stanley. They said the supplier of precision electronics has a durable business model, but the consensus estimate for organic growth may be too optimistic.

Ametek (NYSE:AME) is driven by a “customer-centric flywheel,” whereby early customer feedback informs internal R&D and acquisition decisions, Christopher Snyder, analyst at Morgan Stanley, said in an April 14 report. These innovations, once integrated, are scaled across the company's portfolio of more than 40 distinct business lines, fueling continuous growth.

The model gives Ametek (AME) strong pricing power and resilience in downturns, making it particularly well-positioned as macro growth concerns escalate and new tariffs loom, Snyder noted, referencing the potential impact of renewed U.S. trade restrictions.

Ametek's (AME) industrial brands include Muirhead Avionics, Precitech, Drexelbrook, Dunkermotoren, Gatan, Spectro, Solarton Metrology and Atlas Material Testing Solutions.

Margin strength

While organic growth remains a point of concern, Snyder praised Ametek’s (AME) track record of margin expansion and operational discipline. Since 2019, the company has grown margins by 65 basis points a year, even in the absence of meaningful organic volume growth. This performance, bolstered by consistent and accretive mergers and acquisitions, has led to a compound annual growth rate of 10% for earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization — outpacing peers.

“It is rare and impressive to expand margins through a period of imbalanced M&A versus organic growth,” Snyder wrote.

This margin resilience is expected to help Ametek (AME) defend earnings in the face of Trump 2.0 tariffs, making it a key driver for near-term estimate revisions.

Valuation upside if execution delivers

Morgan Stanley values Ametek (AME) at roughly 23 times forward earnings per share for 2026–2027, representing a 20% premium to the Standard & Poor’s 500 (SP500) stock index and a 15% premium to industry peers. The bank set a price target of $170 a share for Ametek (AME).

This premium is justified, Snyder said, by the firm’s M&A-driven growth algorithm, which targets 8% to 10% top-line growth, split evenly between acquisitions and organic initiatives.

However, he warned that consensus estimates of 5% organic growth from 2025–2027 may be too aggressive, especially given Ametek’s (AME) heavy international exposure (around 50% of revenue), which could be negatively affected by U.S. protectionism.

“Consensus is setting a high bar on organic growth,” Snyder said, pointing to his team’s estimate of more modest organic growth of about 4%.

What could change rating

Morgan Stanley would consider upgrading Ametek (AME) if signs of improved order momentum and a stronger book-to-bill ratio emerge — indicators that the company could meet or exceed organic growth targets. Conversely, a breakdown in the M&A pipeline or unexpected margin pressure could prompt a more bearish outlook.

Despite current concerns, Snyder emphasized that Ametek (AME) remains a high-quality industrial name with significant compounding potential, so long as it can maintain its execution edge and capitalize on acquisition opportunities.

data.title: Ontrak outlines plans to double revenue in 2025 through pipeline expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ontrak, Inc. (OTRK) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Brandon LaVerne announced that Ontrak secured three new regional health plan customers and four health plan expansions over the last 14 months, spanning Medicaid, HARP, commercial, and Medicare Advantage segments. He highlighted the successful launch of the WholeHealth+ solution with Intermountain Health, enrolling 325 members in the first month.
  • LaVerne emphasized the company's strategic shift to being recognized as a value-based care provider, which aligns quality outcomes with financial incentives and provides access to reimbursable fees within medical cost ratios.
  • President and Chief Commercial Officer Mary Louise Osborne discussed the launch of WholeHealth+ for Intermountain’s Medicare Advantage population in Nevada, noting potential future scaling to additional Medicare Advantage cohorts and other business lines.
  • Osborne stated that six new health plan prospects have formally requested financial and clinical proposals, with one large Midwest plan in the late stages of the sales process capable of doubling Ontrak’s run rate revenue.
  • CFO James Park reported that Q4 2024 revenue was $3.1 million, reflecting an 11% year-over-year decrease due to the loss of a customer earlier in the year. He noted sequential increases in revenue per member per month, attributable to newer customer pricing structures and Engage program enrollments.

Outlook

  • Management projected revenues of $2 million to $2.3 million for Q1 2025, citing customer losses and ramp-up phases of new partnerships as reasons for the temporary step-down in revenue.
  • LaVerne expressed confidence in achieving a $15 million run rate by Q2 2025, supported by the onboarding of members from recent launches.
  • The company sees potential to double its revenue in 2025 and again in subsequent years, driven by a robust pipeline and strategic discussions with high-value prospects.

Financial Results

  • Ontrak enrolled a total of 1,641 new members in Q4 2024, including 659 for the Engage program, marking the highest quarterly enrollment since Q3 2021.
  • The company reported a gross margin of 61%, reflecting a slight decrease from the prior quarter and a year-over-year decline driven by pricing mix and new customer enrollments.
  • Operating cash flow for the quarter was negative $4.3 million, compared to negative $1.4 million in Q3 2024, with year-end cash reserves of $5.7 million.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE - No analyst questions were reported in the provided transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were not present, so sentiment assessment is limited to management statements. Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, with phrases like “we are very excited” and “we see a path to double our revenue.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone appeared to remain consistent, emphasizing strategic progress and growth prospects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue declined sequentially from $2.6 million in Q3 2024 to $3.1 million in Q4 2024; however, gross margins fell from 62% to 61%. Enrollment growth significantly increased from 1,166 new members in Q3 to 1,641 in Q4.
  • Strategic focus shifted towards expanding the value-based care model and increasing outreach pools, which grew from 13,168 in Q3 2024 to 29,000 by the end of Q4 2024.
  • Management confidence remains high, continuing to highlight pipeline opportunities and anticipated revenue growth despite temporary setbacks.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company faces challenges related to customer losses and cash flow, with operating cash flow showing a significant deficit.
  • Management acknowledged the potential for further gross margin decreases in the mid-50% range due to pricing and revenue mix changes.
  • Financing discussions are ongoing to secure capital necessary for executing the sales pipeline and business plan.

Final Takeaway

Ontrak, Inc. is navigating challenges from customer losses while focusing on growth through a robust sales pipeline and strategic initiatives. The company foresees doubling its revenue in 2025, supported by its shift to a value-based care model and strong engagement with prospective health plan partners. Despite short-term revenue declines, management remains optimistic about long-term growth opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Netflix aiming for $1T market cap by 2030: report
data.description:
Netflix is aiming to hit a $1T market capitalization and a doubling of its revenue by 2030, The Wall Street Journal reported Monday, according to people familiar with the matter.

According to the Journal, executives shared the revenue, ad sales, and operating income goals for the 2030 target at an annual business review meeting last month, people who attended the meeting said. 

Netflix’s current market cap is around $398.3B. 

The people reportedly said the streamer is aiming to double last year’s $39B in revenue by 2030 and to reach $9B in global ad sales by then. Netflix doesn’t release ad sales figures.

Executives are aiming to triple the company’s $10B in operating operating income in 2024 by 2030, one of the people was quoted as saying. 

Netflix had 301.6M subscribers worldwide at the end of 2024, and it wants that to rise to 410M by the close of 2030, that person reportedly said.

Netflix staff have been told by executives that they intend to focus on attracting subscribers overseas, especially in markets that have high broadband penetration, including Brazil and India, The Journal reported, citing some of the people.

Netflix did not immediately respond to a request for comment by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Canada's Carney pledges to boost defense spending, support local industry if elected
data.description:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney vowed to ramp up the country’s military spending above the NATO target of 2% of GDP within a few years, while overhauling the country’s defense procurement process to reduce reliance on U.S. contractors and strengthen domestic industry.

Speaking at the Bombardier (OTCQX:BDRBF) (OTCQX:BDRAF) (OTCPK:BOMBF) aeronautics plant in Montreal on Monday, Carney said the country must adapt to an “increasingly dangerous and divided world” by significantly reinvesting in its armed forces, according to multiple press reports.

He emphasized that while military spending will rise, it can also serve as an engine of industrial growth and job creation across Canada.

“It’s necessary for our safety,” he said, “but it can be done in a way that elevates our own industries and creates good jobs here in Montreal and across the country.”

He pledged to spend more on the military while supporting Canadian manufacturers, particularly in steel, aluminum and critical minerals.

Currently, Canada spends about 1.3% to 1.4% of GDP on defense, falling short of the NATO minimum commitment of 2%.

Carney, a former central banker of England and Canada, last month became prime minister after succeeding Justin Trudeau as leader of the Liberal Party. Carney called for a federal election to take place on April 28, pitting him mainly against the Conservatives led by Pierre Poilievre.

Canada will reach and exceed 2% defense spending threshold faster than previously planned, Carney pledged. Trudeau had targeted 2032 to meet the goal, but Carney said his timeline is “within a few years.”

To help manage this expanded military investment, Carney proposed the creation of a centralized defense procurement agency. This new body would consolidate expertise from across the government and streamline military purchases, with a clear directive to prioritize Canadian suppliers whenever possible.

Looking to European allies

It would also seek to expand security and trade relationships with European allies in light of what Carney described as increasingly adversarial behavior from the current U.S. administration.

“Our military has been both underfunded and overly reliant on the United States,” he said.

As part of his broader defense platform, Carney outlined plans to modernize Canada’s military with investments in submarines, icebreakers and potentially new fighter jets. He said the existing deal to purchase F-35s from Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) will be reviewed, with the option of sourcing aircraft from European partners instead.

In addition to procurement reform, Carney promised to raise the pay for members of the Canadian Armed Forces and support domestic defense firms in growing their export presence — particularly through free trade agreements Canada holds with Europe and Asia.

The Liberal leader also said his government would reassess federal business spending and shift investments toward defense, clean energy, housing and trade infrastructure.

Carney’s campaign promises aim to close the gap with allies who have criticized Canada’s underperformance on defense spending, while positioning national security as a catalyst for economic development at home.

data.title: Chinese gold ETF inflows so far in April exceed entire Q1 total, WGC says
data.description:
Gold futures edged lower on Monday but remained above $3,200/oz, as the market's risk appetite improved after President Trump exempted smartphones, computers and other electronic items from steep tariffs on China, at least temporarily.

"Perhaps some relief on the tariff front, with the exemption of some electronics maybe taking some of the safe haven bid out, [but] ongoing uncertainty about trade and tariffs, weakness in the dollar and softer yields tend to be supportive for gold," Zaner Metals senior metals strategist Peter Grant said.

Gold's one-day decline is not likely to become a short-term trend, Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities wrote, as "tariff headlines continue to hit the tape, the dollar continues to slide, and the Chinese continue to lurk in the shadows while holding the second most amount of U.S. debt on the planet... That should allow gold to attract enough safe haven flow to at least consolidate within striking distance of the all-time record high."

Gold has been supported by a jump in ETF inflows, a weaker dollar, and safe-haven appeal, but a sharp selloff in the long end of the U.S. Treasury curve also likely has triggered additional gold purchases, SP Angel analysts said, noting gold and Treasurys usually compete for safe-haven status, and investors may be reducing Treasury positions in favor of gold given concerns over the U.S. economy.

Goldman Sachs doubled down on its bullish stance on gold, raising its year-end forecast to $3,700/oz, citing stronger than expected central bank demand and heightened recession risks affecting ETF inflows.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery closed -0.5% to $3,204.80/oz, snapping a four-session winning streak but still the second-highest ever close for the front-month contract, while front-month April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished +0.8% to $32.092/oz, its sixth straight daily gain.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

Investment flows into Chinese physically backed gold ETFs far this month have surpassed flows for the entire Q1 and overtaken inflows registered by U.S.-listed funds, the World Gold Council reported.

Gold ETFs in China increased by 29.1 metric tons in the first 11 days of April, compared with inflows of 23.5 tons during the January-to-March period, WGC senior market strategist John Reade said.

"If the first quarter of this year was dominated by the U.S. tariff-related gold flows and Western ETF buying, the second quarter may have a very different theme, that of a surge in investor interest in gold from China," Reade wrote.

data.title: Thinkific Labs to convert all multiple voting shares to single voting common shares
data.description:
• Thinkific Labs (TSX:THNC:CA) announced on Monday that it will eliminate its dual-class structure by converting all 44,401,620 issued and outstanding multiple voting shares into subordinate voting shares on a one for one basis.
  • Upon completion of this transaction, the company will have 68,027,927 common shares issued and outstanding. With this change, all shareholders will have an equal vote per share, said the company. 
  • Upon approval of the Toronto Stock Exchange, the company anticipates that the common shares will begin trading on the TSX under the same symbol on or about April 21, 2025. 
  • Registered shareholders who hold certificates or DRS (Direct Registration System) advices representing the subordinate voting shares do not need to take any further action, said the company. 
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Bessent eyes fall interviews for next Federal Reserve chair - report
data.description:
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Monday he will launch interviews sometime this fall to select the next Federal Reserve chair.

Current Fed boss Jerome Powell's term is set to end May 15, 2026.

Speaking in a wide-ranging interview with Bloomberg Television, Bessent also noted that the U.S. Treasury Department has enough room to boost buybacks -- "if we wanted" -- should the bond market show further signs of stress. 

Last week, the Treasury chief described last week's bond-market swoon as "uncomfortable but normal deleveraging."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
